data.title: Aiper Wins Kantar's "2025 Pioneering Global Brand" Award, Leading the Global Smart Yard Revolution with AI Innovation
data.description:
Aiper Wins Kantar's "2025 Pioneering Global Brand" Award, Leading the Global Smart Yard Revolution with AI Innovation

Aiper Wins Kantar's "2025 Pioneering Global Brand" Award, Leading the Global Smart Yard Revolution with AI Innovation

PR Newswire

SHANGHAI, Sept. 24, 2025

SHANGHAI, Sept. 24, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- Recently, Kantar, the globally authoritative brand evaluation institution, unveiled the "2025 Pioneering Global Brand" list. Aiper, the global leader in cordless robotic pool cleaners, became the only awardee in the category for its outstanding performance in yard maintenance equipment. This honor not only solidifies Aiper's leading position in the global market but also highlights how its AI-driven smart yard solutions are redefining outdoor living experiences for households worldwide. 

As the pioneer of cordless robotic pool cleaner and a leader in smart yard product solutions, Aiper has consistently focused on its brand mission of "making outdoor living carefree". Since launching its global strategy in 2023, covering markets in North America, Europe, Australia, Latin America, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia, Aiper has provided smart cleaning services to over 2 million families, establishing itself as a leading player in the robotic pool cleaning industry. The strategic partnerships with POOLCORP, the world's largest wholesale distributor of swimming pool and related backyard products, and Fluidra, a global leader in the pool industry, have further strengthened Aiper's brand leadership and global distribution network. 

Kantar's recognition also acknowledges Aiper's robust capabilities in addressing industry challenges through AI innovation. At IFA 2025 in Germany, Aiper launched its first AI-powered robotic pool cleaner, Scuba V3. The product won multiple awards, including the Chip Highlight IFA 2025, Netzwelt Innovation IFA 2025, and Reviewed Awards IFA 2025. Equipped with a built-in camera and object recognition, Scuba V3 is able to detect and target over 20 different types of pool debris - from fine sand and dirt to sticks, ensuring precise coverage and efficient pool cleaning. Compared to traditional robotic pool cleaners, Scuba V3's AI Vision Cleaning capability significantly enhances cleaning efficiency. Weighing just 7.5 kg and featuring a quick-drain design, it's incredibly easy to handle and operate.

This is not Aiper's first technological breakthrough: from the launch of cordless robotic pool cleaners, to the WavePath™ and WaveLine™ technologies of the second-generation, and to the All-in-one cleaning capability of the third-generation Scuba X1 Pro Max, Aiper has consistently driven industry evolution through technological innovation. The launch of Scuba V3 marks the transition from "mechanized" to "intelligent" pool cleaning. With one-click activation, users can now bid farewell to the tedious work of pool maintenance and enjoy a truly intelligent, carefree, vacation-like lifestyle.

What Aiper has achieved cannot be separated from its "global vision + deep localization" strategy. In view of the varying consumer habits in Europe, America, and Australia, Aiper's local teams conduct in-depth, on-the-ground market research each year, and gather real-world usage scenarios to ensure product design and value delivery are precisely aligned with local user needs.

Aiper's vision extends beyond pools. Leveraging its technological expertise in smart cleaning, Aiper is rapidly expanding into smart yard solutions, including smart irrigation. In 2025, Aiper launched IrriSense - a revolutionary lawn care solution with intelligent, water-efficient irrigation. It can adjust irrigation schedule based on real-time weather data, allowing for significant water conservation and supporting sustainable development through green technology. This product has also won multiple awards, including the CES Innovation Awards 2025 and the IFA 2025 Innovation Honoree Award.

With its "tech aesthetics" as well as "smart and practical functions", Aiper's diverse portfolio of smart yard products has earned numerous awards from top global media outlets and world-renowned design institutions, as well as recognition from millions of users worldwide. In the future, pool and yard equipment will work in synergy to build a fully automated smart yard ecosystem that frees users from mundane maintenance tasks, allowing them to fully enjoy the beauty of nature and spend quality time with loved ones.

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/aiper-wins-kantars-2025-pioneering-global-brand-award-leading-the-global-smart-yard-revolution-with-ai-innovation-302565720.html

SOURCE Aiper

data.title: 'Jimmy Kimmel Live!' returned to network TV. -2-
data.description:
Federal employees - everyone from FBI agents to postal workers - do have protections when it comes to freedom of speech. "If you are a federal employee and you believe that you have been discriminated against because of your political affiliation, you may be entitled to some protections under the Civil Service Reform Act of 1978," Katz Banks Kumin says.

On NBC's (CMCSA) "Meet the Press" on Sunday, Sen. Rand Paul, a Republican representing Kentucky, said that the comments made by Carr, in his capacity as chair of the FCC, that seemingly threatened action against Disney were "absolutely inappropriate." But regarding comments made by workers, Paul added: "You have the right to say them, but you don't have the right to employment."

Whether you are a diehard MAGA supporter or a lifelong Democrat, be careful what you tweet and say in a public forum, especially if you work in the private sector. It could come back to haunt you. You may find yourself looking for another job - without a reference - if you are fired for your political opinions in a state that does not have legal protections for such speech.

Whether you are a diehard MAGA supporter or a lifelong Democrat, be careful what you tweet and say in a public forum.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

What is the worst financial mistake you've ever made?

Are you middle class? No, you're not. Here's why.

Why does President Trump want a rate cut so badly?

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-24-25 0630ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: 'Jimmy Kimmel Live!' returned to network TV. Other outspoken American workers may not be so fortunate.
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

Yes, your employer can fire you for your views on Charlie Kirk and Jimmy Kimmel

The First Amendment does not protect private-sector workers who voice their political opinions from being fired. (Pictured: Charlie Kirk, Jimmy Kimmel and President Trump.)

Are you a MAGA supporter? A liberal Democrat? An independent? Or something else? Wherever you fall on the political spectrum, listen up.

Late-night TV host Jimmy Kimmel's case provides a complicated but useful lesson. There has been much debate about what people may be missing when it comes to the decision by ABC, the network owned by Walt Disney Co., to pull Kimmel's show following remarks he made about the murder of conservative activist Charlie Kirk.

Disney's action raised concerns about freedom of speech; drew protestors to the company's (DIS) studio in Burbank, Calif. and resulted in Disney+ subscribers vowing to cancel the streaming service.

Media commentators, citing the First Amendment's protection of free speech from government interference, were quick to point out that the decision to yank the show occurred after pressure from President Donald Trump and the Federal Communications Commission.

Nexstar Media Group (NXST), which operates 33 ABC affiliates, said it would pull "Jimmy Kimmel Live!" over the host's comments. Other affiliates had reportedly threatened to do the same. ABC has around 250 affiliates nationwide.

On Monday, Disney announced that Kimmel's show would return to television on Tuesday. "Last Wednesday, we made the decision to suspend production on the show to avoid further inflaming a tense situation at an emotional moment for our country," the company said in a statement.

Under the First Amendment, the government cannot compel or restrict a worker's speech, but a private-sector employer can fire a worker for their words or actions.

"It is a decision we made because we felt some of the comments were ill-timed and thus insensitive," Disney added. "We have spent the last days having thoughtful conversations with Jimmy, and after those conversations, we reached the decision to return the show on Tuesday."

In his opening monologue on Tuesday evening, Kimmel said, "This show is not important. What's important is that we get to live in a country that allows us to have a show like this."

Trump posted on his social-media platform Truth Social that Kimmel was "yet another arm of the DNC," and suggested he would not back down on his wish to remove Kimmel from the air, adding, "I think we're going to test ABC out on this."

The president added, "Let's see how we do. Last time I went after them, they gave me $16 Million Dollars. This one sounds even more lucrative." (Last December, ABC agreed to give $15 million to Donald Trump's presidential library to settle a defamation lawsuit.)

'This show is not important. What's important is that we get to live in a country that allows us to have a show like this.'Jimmy Kimmel's opening monologue on Tuesday evening

The FCC's Carr said: "We can do this the easy way or the hard way. These companies can find ways to change conduct and take action, frankly, on Kimmel, or you know, there's going to be additional work for the FCC ahead." Critics on both sides of the political aisle have alleged that this violates the First Amendment, which protects private speech from government censorship and discrimination.

The American Civil Liberties Union took a dim view on Carr's remarks: "The government may not retaliate against people or groups because they are criticizing someone's political views, especially when the government is trying to silence views it doesn't like," it said. "The First Amendment prohibits the government from punishing speech even when it is controversial or offensive."

Attorney General Pam Bondi, meanwhile, made a sweeping statement that the government will pursue people who post "hate speech." In a podcast interview, she said: "There's free speech, and then there's hate speech," adding, "We will absolutely target you." The Supreme Court has repeatedly ruled that the First Amendment protects most hate speech. Bondi later wrote on X that hate speech that "crosses the line into threats of violence is NOT protected."

But while the First Amendment applies to government censorship - the government cannot compel or restrict a worker's speech - a private-sector employer can fire an employee for their words or actions.

The rights of federal employees are protected under the First Amendment as long as the person is acting as a private citizen, and their comments relate to issues of public concern and are not made as part of their official duties. "Those rights are limited in the workplace to balance your right to speak with the government's interest in maintaining an efficient, disruption-free workforce," according to the Patriots Law Group in Suitland, Md.

Related: Jimmy Kimmel supporters who pushed for Disney boycott take victory lap over his return to ABC

The First Amendment

Case in point: The U.S. Secret Service put an agent on leave who allegedly wrote a negative Facebook post about Kirk. In a statement, the Secret Service said it "will not tolerate behavior that violates our code of conduct. This employee was immediately put on administrative leave, and an investigation has begun."

Trump, speaking on Sunday evening at the 63,400-seat State Farm Stadium in Glendale, Ariz., praised Kirk as a "great American hero." Trump said: "He's a martyr now for America's freedom. I know I speak for everyone here today when I say that none of us will ever forget Charlie. And neither now will history."

The dilemma for employees and employers across the country is that Americans have varying views on Kirk. If your boss sees footage of you at the memorial service for Kirk and they disagree with Kirk's or Trump's political views, they can fire you. Freedom of speech cuts both ways, politically speaking.

The hard truth is that employees can be fired in the U.S. for expressing their political opinions. But the legal framework at the federal and state levels is a complex one, and an employer's ability to fire someone without facing a wrongful-dismissal lawsuit also depends on the nature of the political speech and where it occurred.

Don't miss: Is it time to start worrying about a U.S. recession (again)?

Managers field complaints

Andrew Kragie, an associate at the law firm Maynard Nexsen who specializes in employment law, says private-sector employees often misunderstand how freedom of speech is protected at work. "Managers regularly field complaints about what employees say at work, from provocative political opinions to religious evangelism," he says.

The First Amendment does not apply to private employers. Kragie says the most important federal law protecting workers and freedom of speech is the National Labor Relations Act, which is very specific in allowing employees to discuss and organize around wages, working conditions and unionization.

The National Labor Relations Act "protects the rights of employees to act together to address conditions at work, with or without a union," he says, but it does not address politics. It does, however, extend to workplace conversations related to workers' conditions that may take place on social-media platforms such as Facebook (META), Twitter or TikTok, or at the office water cooler.

Whether you are a diehard MAGA supporter or a lifelong Democrat, be careful what you tweet and say in a public forum, especially if you work in the private sector.

There are also state-level laws. New York, California and the District of Columbia, for example, offer legal protections against discrimination based on political affiliation, although the scope and specific protections vary. Political beliefs are not protected under federal law. Specifically, New York and D.C. prohibit employers from refusing to hire, fire or discriminate against workers for their political activities.

Florida, meanwhile, is an at-will employment state, meaning you can be fired for any reason or no reason at all. While California is also an at-will state, California Labor Code Section 1101 states that no employer can prevent or forbid an employee from participating in politics or from becoming a candidate for public office.

Section 201-D of the New York State Labor Law prohibits discrimination based on "political activities outside of working hours, off of the employer's premises and without the use of the employer's equipment or other property." This law applies to private-sector workers.

Don't miss: Did baby boomers really have it easier than millennials?

Kaleidoscope of laws

The U.S. has a kaleidoscope of laws governing political speech. There are no federal protections from workplace discrimination related to political affiliation for private-sector employees, says law firm Katz Banks Kumin. Louisiana, for example, prohibits private employers from adopting policies or attempting to control, direct or influence workers' political affiliations or activities.

Every state has its own protections for employees. "Other states have less restrictive laws, such as Kentucky, which more narrowly tailors its laws towards preventing employers from influencing or retaliating against an individual's voting activity," Katz Banks Kumin adds. "Local and/or county ordinances may also apply in some instances."

Alabama, Alaska, Arkansas, Florida, Kansas, Maine, Maryland (excluding some counties), Michigan, Mississippi, New Hampshire, New Jersey, North Carolina, Oklahoma, Pennsylvania, South Dakota, Texas, Vermont, Virginia and Wisconsin do not have specific protections against discrimination based on political affiliation in private employment, the law firm says.

The National Labor Relations Act is a powerful, but highly specific, law. It extends to workplace conversations related to labor conditions, but it does not extend to politics.

(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

09-24-25 0630ET

Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: North American Morning Briefing: Investors Weigh Powell Comments, Await Inflation Data
data.description:
OPENING CALL

Stock futures edged higher on Wednesday as investors were taking their cue from Federal Reserve Chair Powell's comments on balancing concerns around the labor market and inflation.

The Fed's preferred inflation gauge is due this week, along with home-sales data and earnings from warehouse retailer Costco.

Friday's PCE inflation data will be crucial, as a softer reading could bolster the case for further monetary easing.

Stocks to Watch

Alibaba's shares looked set for a sharp rise Wednesday after it unveiled plans to boost investment in AI to more than $53 billion. Its Hong Kong-listed shares rose 9%.

Aytu BioPharma reported revenue below Wall Street's expectations, and a wider loss than anticipated. The stock fell 14% postmarket.

Cohu plans to offer $200 million in convertible senior notes due in 2031. Shares declined 10% after-hours.

Cyclerion Therapeutics entered a licensing agreement with MIT securing IP for its strategic relaunch. The stock rose 39%.

General Motors and Lithium Americas : The U.S. is negotiating, according to a media report, for a stake of up to 10% in Lithium Americas, which is developing a mine with GM. Shares of GM rose 3% premarket, while Lithium Americas shot up by three-quarters.

Kenvue shares looked poised to build on Tuesday's gains. They hit a record low on Monday after Trump said the painkiller's active ingredient might cause autism.

Micron's revenue jumped 46%, driven by demand from AI developers. Its outlook for this quarter also topped forecasts. Shares rose roughly 2% premarket.

Watch For:

New Home Sales for August, EIA Weekly Petroleum Status Report, Earnings from Cintas

Today's Top Headlines/Must Reads:

- The Doctors Building a Public-Health Universe Outside the Government

- Inside Trump's Chaotic Overhaul of the H-1B Visa

- A Closer Look at the Drug Trump Is Touting for Autism

MARKET WRAPS

Forex:

The dollar rose after Powell's comments.

The currency appeared to be under significant pressure due to weaker growth prospects, policy risks and narrowing interest-rate differentials, MFS IM said.

"The only silver lining is that we do not believe that the dollar will lose its status as the primary reserve currency in the foreseeable future."

The euro extended its losses against the dollar after data showed German business confidence unexpectedly fell in September.

Trump's latest comments on the Russia-Ukraine war have raised the risk of the euro falling, ING said.

While his comments have had little impact on the euro , they posed "downside risks" for the single currency, it said.

Bonds:

Treasury yields declined, extending the previous day's moves following Powell's comments.

He said he saw "no risk-free path," prompting demand for Treasurys.

A weak economic growth outlook favors a slightly longer duration within bond portfolios, ODDO BHF said.

"Given the subdued economic growth in the U.S. and Europe, we believe a slightly longer duration makes sense."

Energy:

Risks of disruption to Russian supplies drove oil prices higher.

"Traders remain caught between geopolitical tensions and oversupply concerns, leaving oil little changed overall this month," MUFG said.

Metals:

Gold prices eased, weighed down by a firmer dollar.

"Growing expectations of deeper Fed rate cuts along with mounting geopolitical and economic uncertainty are fuelling this price rally," ANZ said.

Gold Chart

Comex gold futures were poised to test resistance at $3,800/oz, RHB Retail Research said, according to the daily chart .

Silver

Silver futures could rally to $46/oz, or even higher, by 2Q 2026, according to Commonwealth Bank of Australia.

Iron

Iron ore's recent price rally appeared overstretched , Citi Research said. 

   TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

TotalEnergies to Invest Around $5.3 Billion in French Wind Farm After Winning Tender

French energy major TotalEnergies said it was chosen alongside Germany's RWE to build and operate an offshore wind farm in France.

The project will represent an investment of around 4.5 billion euros ($5.32 billion) for the design, building and operation of the Centre Manche 2 site, marking the company's largest investment in France in 30 years, it said Wednesday.

Wall Street Is Poaching Bankers in a Red-Hot Job Market

A pickup in dealmaking and initial public offerings is helping fuel a hot job market on Wall Street.

Big banks had been adding staff over the past year in strategic expansions, but now sudden jumps in activity have them seeking to hire even more and slowing layoffs they might have otherwise executed.

Get Rich or Get Wiped Out: Bitcoin's Hottest New Trade

The market for cryptocurrencies is known for boom-and-bust trades. It is about to get even wilder.

Traders seeking rapid returns have made a speculative bitcoin play one of the most popular crypto bets globally: so-called perpetual futures. These potentially offer returns of 10, 20 or even 100 times an initial investment-or huge losses that could leave a trader with nothing.

Stephen Miran's Rate-Cut Arguments Don't Add Up

After voting for a supersize rate cut last week, Stephen Miran has now provided the intellectual cover for President Trump's calls for drastically lower interest rates. If Miran is right, everyone else is wrong-not just the Federal Reserve, but investors and independent economists too.

Miran, who joined the Fed board just before the rate cut and is on leave of absence as the head of Trump's Council of Economic Advisers, wants rates almost 2 percentage points lower, at 2.5%-against the 4% to 4.25% range following last week's reduction.

German Companies Turn Gloomier as Economy Flounders

Confidence among German businesses fell back for the first time this year, according to a survey that adds to worrying signs for Europe's most important economy.

The Ifo Institute said Wednesday that its business-climate index declined to 87.7 in September from 88.9 last month. That decrease breaks a streak of gradually improving sentiment among German companies unbroken since the start of 2025, and overturns economists' expectations for a further uptick in the index.

China to Stop Seeking Special WTO Treatment in Future Talks

China said it would no longer seek new special treatment for developing countries in current and future World Trade Organization negotiations, signaling a possible effort to ease a longstanding friction point with the U.S. ahead of a planned summit.

Chinese Premier Li Qiang announced the shift on Tuesday at a meeting of China's Global Development Initiative on the sidelines of the United Nations General Assembly in New York, according to state-run Xinhua News Agency.

Camp Mystic, Devastated by Floods, to Reopen Next Year

Camp Mystic is set to reopen next year after summer floods killed more than two dozen campers and counselors at the Texas girls camp.

The camp's owners said in emails The Wall Street Journal viewed Tuesday that it was planning to open its Cypress Lake location next summer but not its site along the Guadalupe River, which overflowed during rainstorms over the Fourth of July weekend. Water rushed into cabins and devastated the Christian girls camp and surrounding areas in Texas Hill Country. Parents of campers have been split over whether the camp should reopen.

Milei Is Counting on Trump to Bail Argentina Out of an Economic Mess

Argentine President Javier Milei is staking the future of his free-market overhaul on his relationship with President Trump, who stopped short of promising a bailout for Argentina but issued an unusual political endorsement of the leader in coming elections.

"We're going to help them," Trump told reporters Tuesday after meeting with Milei on the sidelines of the United Nations General Assembly gathering of world leaders. "If he can continue to do the job that he's been doing, it's going to really be something special."

Write to priscila.barrera@wsj.com

TODAY IN CANADA

Earnings:

AGF Management 3Q

Economic Indicators (ET):

Nothing major scheduled

data.title: People Diagnosed with Lung Cancer Aren't Getting Vital Tests; New Campaign Hopes to Change That
data.description:
People Diagnosed with Lung Cancer Aren't Getting Vital Tests; New Campaign Hopes to Change That

People Diagnosed with Lung Cancer Aren't Getting Vital Tests; New Campaign Hopes to Change That

PR Newswire

CHICAGO, Sept. 24, 2025

American Lung Association launches campaign to address barriers to lung cancer screening and care

CHICAGO, Sept. 24, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- In the U.S., only 16% of people eligible for lung cancer screening are getting the lifesaving scan, and 23% of lung cancer patients receive chemotherapy and/or radiation before undergoing full biomarker testing, potentially resulting in less successful treatment. Today, the American Lung Association launched a campaign to raise awareness about lung cancer screening and biomarker testing, shaped by the voices of people diagnosed with lung cancer and their caregivers across the country.

"Every year, 235,000 people are diagnosed with lung cancer. While lung cancer remains the leading cause of cancer deaths in the U.S., the five-year survival rate improved 26% in recent years to 28.4%. People are living longer with the disease because of greater access to early lung cancer screening coupled with transformative breakthroughs in research and diagnostics," said Harold Wimmer, President and CEO of the American Lung Association. "Unfortunately, not enough people are getting these tests, so we went right to the source – people with lung cancer and their families – to find out why. We then crafted this new campaign based on their feedback to make sure more people get screened for lung cancer and receive information about the pathway to diagnosis and comprehensive biomarker testing."

When caught early, lung cancer can often be treated with surgery alone, and the likelihood of surviving five years or more improves to 65%. Individuals who are age 50-80 years old, who currently smoke, or who have quit smoking within the last 15 years, and have a 20-pack year smoking history (1 pack a day for two years, 2 packs a day for 10 years, etc.), are eligible for low- or no-cost screening. Once lung cancer is suspected on a scan, minimally invasive diagnostic procedures and comprehensive biomarker testing are critical to identify next steps and treatment options upon diagnosis. Also known as Next Generation Sequencing, or NGS testing, biomarker testing looks for key markers in a tumor's DNA. Some lung cancer treatments target these characteristics directly, often with fewer side effects and better outcomes.

To better understand barriers to optimal lung cancer care, including access to and an understanding of screening and diagnostic procedures, the Lung Association conducted focus groups with caregivers and individuals living with lung cancer. Key focus group findings include:

  • High Anxiety Due to Lack of Information: People reported feeling overwhelmed and unprepared after learning they had a lung nodule following lung cancer screening. Many were unclear about what it meant and wanted more upfront information from healthcare providers.
  • Communication Breakdowns: Participants described poor or inconsistent communication both between healthcare providers and in conversations with patients, leading to confusion and distress.
  • Misunderstanding of "Watchful Waiting:" When told a lung nodule would be monitored over time, people often didn't understand what that entailed, increasing fears about potential cancer progression.
  • Inconsistent Diagnostic Processes: There was significant variability in how providers assessed whether a nodule was cancerous. People in the focus group and surveys wanted more transparency and direction throughout the process.

This new campaign responds directly to those concerns, offering clear, evidence-based resources to people about lung cancer screening and biomarker testing. For this campaign, the Lung Association partnered with Guardant to enhance and expand patient education tools and resources. In addition, the Lung Association continues to actively promote the Lung Cancer Basics training course, and Lung Cancer Support Groups and other support resources.

For more information, visit Lung.org/lungcancer. 

Support for this campaign is provided by Guardant Health.

About the American Lung Association
The American Lung Association is the leading organization working to save lives by improving lung health and preventing lung disease through education, advocacy and research. The work of the American Lung Association is focused on four strategic imperatives: to defeat lung cancer; to champion clean air for all; to improve the quality of life for those with lung disease and their families; and to create a tobacco-free future. For more information about the American Lung Association, which has a 4-star rating from Charity Navigator and is a Platinum-Level GuideStar Member, call 1-800-LUNGUSA (1-800-586-4872) or visit: Lung.org. To support the work of the American Lung Association, find a local event at Lung.org/events or donate today at Lung.org/donate. 

American Lung Association • 55 W. Wacker Drive, Suite 1150 • Chicago, IL 60601
1331 Pennsylvania Ave. NW, Ste. 1425 North • Washington, D.C. 20004
1-800-LUNGUSA (1-800-586-4872) Lung.org

CONTACT: Jill Dale | American Lung Association
P: 312-940-7001 M: 720-438-8289 E: Jill.Dale@Lung.org

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/people-diagnosed-with-lung-cancer-arent-getting-vital-tests-new-campaign-hopes-to-change-that-302564976.html

SOURCE American Lung Association

data.title: Jaguar Land Rover Working to Pay Suppliers After Hack, Bloomberg Says, Citing Sources
data.description:
--Jaguar Land Rover is trying to clear a backlog of payments owed to suppliers after a cyberattack halted production at the carmaker's factories, Bloomberg says, citing people familiar with the matter.

--The company aims to ensure that suppliers have liquidity and can retain workers, enabling them to quickly ramp production back up when JLR resumes output, the people said, according to Bloomberg.

--JLR has paid roughly 300 million pounds ($405.7 million) to partners and aims to clear the backlog by the end of the month, Bloomberg said, citing one of the people.

--"Our focus remains on supporting our customers, suppliers, colleagues, and our retailers who remain open. We fully recognize this is a difficult time for all connected with JLR and we thank everyone for their continued support and patience," a JLR spokesperson said to Bloomberg. 




  Full story: https://tinyurl.com/vvbnw9nr 





  Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 24, 2025 06:09 ET (10:09 GMT)

data.title: MetaOptics Deploys Direct Laser Writer in Taiwan to Power Prototyping and Production
data.description:
MetaOptics Deploys Direct Laser Writer in Taiwan to Power Prototyping and Production

MetaOptics Deploys Direct Laser Writer in Taiwan to Power Prototyping and Production

PR Newswire

SINGAPORE, Sept. 24, 2025

SINGAPORE, Sept. 24, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- MetaOptics Ltd (Catalist: 9MT), a leading-edge semiconductor optics company, has deployed its Direct Laser Writer system at Pin-Jye Nano Technologies Co., Ltd. in Taoyuan, Taiwan. The installation brings metalens prototyping and fabrication closer to the regionʼs semiconductor supply chain and marks an important step in MetaOpticsʼ global expansion.

By building up local development capabilities, MetaOptics will help partners iterate faster and move from concept to product more efficiently. The Taoyuan site will also serve as a launchpad for scaling up production and collaboration. Additional Direct Laser Writers and automatic testers are planned to expand capacity and meet growing demand for metalenses. As part of these efforts, MetaOptics is developing metalens solutions for Co-Packaged Optics (CPO), with demonstrated nanofabrication capability down to 150nm or lesser. CPO has emerged as a powerful technology, to revolutionize AI infrastructure by shifting from copper to light-based data transmission. MetaOptics' metalens arrays make optical receivers and transceivers smaller than traditional micro-optics, enabling more compact and scalable systems.

"Taiwan sits at the heart of the semiconductor world. This deployment brings us closer to our partners and positions us to support them with faster turnaround, better integration, and a path to scale," said Mark Thng, Executive Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of MetaOptics Ltd.

Beyond expanding metalens production capacity in Taiwan, MetaOptics is also exploring a presence in the United States to better serve key customers. To support this growth, MetaOptics has secured long lead components to enable faster replication of its Direct Laser Writers. MetaOptics' Direct Laser Writer achieves a minimum line width of 90nm, a level of precision unmatched by competing systems.

Looking ahead, in collaboration with major semiconductor OEMs, MetaOptics aims to further advance its Direct Laser Writer technology to handle 12-inch formats with simultaneous multi-lens writing. This maskless process, when combined with multi-lens writing, is expected to dramatically increase throughput and unlock new opportunities for scaling.

These efforts are part of MetaOpticsʼ vision to make metalens technologies more accessible by simplifying how metalenses are designed, tested, and brought into the real world. With the delivery of the Direct Laser Writer, the Company expects its revenue in FY2025 to be materially higher than in FY2024.

About MetaOptics Ltd

MetaOptics Ltd (Catalist: 9MT) is a leading-edge semiconductor optics company pioneering glass-based metalens solutions enhanced by AI-driven image processing. Using advanced optical design and a scalable 12-inch DUV lithography process, it powers next-generation applications in CPO, mobile, AR VR, automotive and other emerging markets. Headquartered in Singapore, MetaOptics aims to deliver high-performance optics with the reliability and scalability demanded by today's most innovative technology brands. For sales inquiries, please contact sales@metaoptics.sg.

For media inquiries, please contact: ir@metaoptics.sg

 

 

 

View original content:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/metaoptics-deploys-direct-laser-writer-in-taiwan-to-power-prototyping-and-production-302565204.html

SOURCE METAOPTICS LTD

data.title: In The Room Launches Innovative Technology to Maximize Online Event ROI
data.description:
LAS VEGAS, NV / ACCESS Newswire / September 24, 2025 / In The Room, a technology company specializing in online event registration, tracking, and data-driven solutions, today announced the expansion of its platform to help organizers maximize event ROI and uncover new revenue streams. Built on years of hands-on event experience, the platform empowers hosts to better understand audience engagement, increase conversions, and ensure no opportunity is left on the table.

Founded by Bill Allen, CEO of 7 Figure Flipping, and Blaze Faillaci, COO, In The Room emerged from a simple yet costly problem: at large-scale events, organizers could track attendance and purchases, but not who actually heard critical offers. Without this insight, many potential sales were lost-not because attendees weren't interested, but because they weren't present at the exact moment an offer was delivered.

Determined to solve this gap, Allen and Faillaci teamed up with developer Taylor Harry, who brought over a decade of software development expertise. Together, they built a scalable online event registration software integrated with RFID tracking technology to deliver real-time attendee engagement data. This innovation ensures organizers can not only track attendee counts but also pinpoint who heard each offer-transforming how live events drive revenue.

Driving Real ROI for Event Leaders

After proving its system internally, In The Room rolled out the technology to trusted partners. At Russell Brunson's Funnel Hacking Live, their registration and live event data tracking system contributed to an additional $2.8 million in sales. Since then, the company has powered events for industry leaders including Pace Morby's Squad Up Summit.

With features such as online event lead retrieval, event lead capture software, and real-time data collection, In The Room provides end-to-end solutions for conferences of all sizes.

Built for Growth and Monetization

The company's platform is designed to:

  • Improve event ROI by helping organizers understand attendee behavior and optimize conversion opportunities.

  • Scale effortlessly from small webinars to large online conferences with thousands of participants.

  • Provide seamless experiences with plug-and-play systems that reduce stress for organizers while enhancing attendee satisfaction.

As the live events industry continues to evolve, In The Room is positioning itself as a partner for hosts, speakers, and conference organizers looking to increase event ROI and explore how to make money from online events more effectively leveraging event analytics software.

About In The Room

In The Room is a data-driven event technology company founded by Bill Allen, Blaze Faillaci, and Taylor Harry. The company delivers scalable registration systems, RFID tracking, and real-time engagement analytics designed to help organizers maximize event ROI. With proven success at events such as Funnel Hacking Live and Squad Up Summit, In The Room provides innovative tools to capture leads, improve conversions, and transform the way online events generate revenue.

Media Contact:

In The Room

Blaze@intheroom.live

SOURCE: In The Room



View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: I'm 70 and have Alzheimer's. My son's girlfriend put $750 worth of lottery tickets on a credit card I gave him. What can I do?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'I contacted the credit-card company, but was advised they could not reimburse me for the theft'

"I have always paid his real-estate taxes, and auto-insurance premiums on two vehicles." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

I am 70 years old and living with Alzheimer's disease. I live in Florida.

My son lives in Oregon. I have a wonderful spouse who takes good care of me and cooks all meals, etc. My son is my only offspring and he is slightly autistic. He is 50 years old. He receives financial benefits from his stint in the Army. However, I have always paid his real-estate taxes, and auto-insurance premiums on two vehicles. I have also provided him with a credit card to pay for unexpected expenses above his financial needs. I have also paid for substantial repairs to his home. He has never used the card for frivolous things.

Growing up in severe poverty, I have amassed a substantial sum of money. I saved over $1 million, in addition to buying real estate so that I can die knowing my son will have financial backing. However, I now see that is possibly the worst decision I can make. My spouse and I have always kept our finances separate. My son has a live-in girlfriend. I was always happy he had someone in his life and we got along well. However, she recently purchased $750 in lotto tickets using my credit card that I provide for emergencies.

I contacted the credit-card company, but was advised they could not reimburse me for the theft because she lives in the home and has access to the card. My son was very angry that I might charge her for theft or do anything adverse to her. He said he would never see nor speak to me again if I proceeded with legal action. I know this is elder abuse because of my age and my Alzheimer's is getting worse. I am certain I will need home care or nursing-home care in the future. But what should I do about my son?

The Mother

Related: I'm about to inherit a lot of money. How do I make sure my husband doesn't get any of it?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

Cut off the credit card. It's time for your son to stand on his own two feet.

Dear Mother,

If she did it once, she will do it again. As will he.

Cut off the credit card. It's time for your son to stand on his own. If he can afford to have a house, car and girlfriend, he can afford to pay his own bills, property taxes, auto insurance and dinners in or out with his girlfriend. Your trust has been broken and, rather than apologizing and attempting to make amends, he threatened you with the only gunpowder he has in his cannon: the withdrawal of his love. But love that is conditional and used to financially exploit an elderly parent who has dementia is not the kind of love you should accept as worthy of you.

You are fortunate to have a loving spouse, so you can have a long conversation about how you would like your assets to be managed after you go - and one way to do that is to act now, while you still have testamentary capacity, to set up a marital trust with a spendthrift clause. It's important to act as soon as possible. You can, if you wish, set up payments for your son for property taxes and other bills, but it's questionable whether supporting him financially has a) earned you his respect and loyalty and b) helped him or merely enabled him.

When you have established a trust, which will become irrevocable upon your passing, you can also look into setting up power-of-attorney documents for your spouse. The power of attorney should be "durable" and should specify that your partner will make decisions on your behalf when you are incapacitated or otherwise unable to do so yourself. Until that time, you will maintain the power to make your own decisions. You should also assign your spouse and/or a third party to have power of attorney for healthcare decisions.

A living will or living trust can also provide additional instructions for how you wish your estate to be handled after your death. Crucially, a trust would help your executor to avoid probate, and also provide conditions for any future payments made to your son. Making sure you have these documents in place before you enter mid- or late-stage Alzheimer's will prevent complications later on - like your spouse having to apply to be your guardian or conservator, which can be a lengthy and complicated process.

Preventing elder financial abuse

The National Institute on Aging outlines several signs that a person is no longer able to look after their own finances, including "trouble counting change, paying for a purchase, calculating a tip, balancing a checkbook or understanding a bank statement." Nearly 7 million Americans have Alzheimer's disease, which is one of many forms of dementia. Nearly two-thirds of people with Alzheimer's are women, and older Black Americans are about twice as likely as white Americans to have this disease.

Elder financial abuse, researchers say, falls into the categories of "occasion, desperation and predation" - or opportunity, financial need and strategic exploitation. It only takes one bad actor to create a problem, and you can remove any temptation from your son by giving him some tough love. Roughly 10% of people over 65 experience some form of abuse, according to studies cited by a paper published in the Cureus, a peer-reviewed online medical journal. "These rates are often higher in long-term-care facilities such as nursing homes, despite government regulations aimed toward addressing this issue," the authors wrote.

Ordinarily, if you suspect someone of elder abuse, you should report them to adult protective services, or call 911 and report them to local law-enforcement authorities or your district attorney's office. In this case, your son took advantage of your generosity, and has shown that he is not above using emotional blackmail to get what he wants. The National Center on Elder Abuse, a government agency affiliated with the U.S. Administration on Aging, says that 1 in 10 people over the age of 60 in the U.S. experienced some form of abuse in the prior year.

You can also freeze your account with all three major credit bureaus - Experian (UK:EXPN), TransUnion (TRU) and Equifax (EFX) - so your son and/or his girlfriend are unable to take out loans or open accounts in your name. This is also good practice for anyone whose financial accounts or Social Security number have been compromised. MetLife has found that women were nearly twice as likely as men to be victims of elder financial abuse. Most victims were over the age of 80, lived alone and required some level of help with either healthcare or home maintenance.

Your son needs to experience life without the gravy train.

Related: My mother, 81, discovered her 'millions' in investments are gone. What can I do?

Previous columns by Quentin Fottrell:

My friend, 61, signed a $20K contract for dental implants. She can't afford it. Can she fight it?

My married adult son constantly demands money. How do I put an end to his mooching?

My friend married for a second time. Will the kids from her first marriage lose their inheritance?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to the Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on the Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell
